OT4ML
Optimal Transport for Machine Learners
Chapters
Optimal Matching between Point CloudsMonge Problem between MeasuresKantorovich RelaxationDual ProblemSemi-discrete and Wasserstein-1Divergences and Dual NormsEntropic Regularization: Sinkhorn AlgorithmEntropic Regularization: ConvergenceGeneralized Wasserstein DistancesGeneralized OT ProblemsBeyond Comparing MeasuresDynamic Optimal TransportWasserstein Gradient FlowsGenerative Models via Transportation
ConclusionNotation TableIndex

Monge Problem between Measures

The goal of this chapter is to pass from finite matching to transport between arbitrary probability laws. The central stakes are to define measures, push-forwards and Monge maps carefully enough that the discrete picture survives, while exposing why deterministic maps can fail to exist. Monge’s original formulation Monge, 1781 and modern treatments Villani, 2003Villani, 2009Santambrogio, 2015Rachev & Rüschendorf, 1998 are the conceptual background for this transition.

The previous chapter handled two sets with the same number of points. To relax this to a more general setting, one needs probability distributions, so that points may carry unequal masses and continuous densities can be treated in the same language as finite clouds.

from pathlib import Path
import sys

from IPython.display import Image as DisplayImage
from IPython.display import display

here = Path.cwd()
myst_dir = None
for candidate in [here, here.parent, here / "myst", here.parent / "myst", here.parent.parent / "myst"]:
    if (candidate / "ot4ml_web.py").exists():
        myst_dir = candidate.resolve()
        sys.path.insert(0, str(myst_dir))
        break

if myst_dir is None:
    raise RuntimeError("Could not locate myst/ot4ml_web.py")

repo_root = myst_dir.parent
thumbnails = repo_root / "notebooks-figures" / "thumbnails"

def show_book_figure(name, width=760):
    display(DisplayImage(filename=str(thumbnails / f"{name}.png"), width=width))

Measures¶

Measures are the language that lets point clouds, densities and singular objects be handled uniformly. We only recall the facts needed later: integration, total variation, densities and probabilistic laws.

Histograms¶

We use the terms histogram and probability vector interchangeably for any element a∈Δna \in \simplex_na∈Δn​ of the probability simplex

Δn:={a∈R+n  :  ∑i=1nai=1}.\simplex_n \eqdef \left\{a \in \RR_+^n \;:\; \sum_{i=1}^n a_i=1\right\}.Δn​:={a∈R+n​:i=1∑n​ai​=1}.
(1)

Discrete And Empirical Measures¶

A discrete measure with weights aaa and locations x1,…,xn∈Xx_1,\ldots,x_n\in\Xx1​,…,xn​∈X reads

α=∑i=1naiδxi,\al = \sum_{i=1}^n a_i \delta_{x_i},α=i=1∑n​ai​δxi​​,
(2)

where δx\delta_xδx​ is the Dirac mass at position xxx, intuitively a unit of mass infinitely concentrated at location xxx. Such a measure is a probability measure if a∈Δna\in\simplex_na∈Δn​, and more generally a positive measure if all weights aia_iai​ are nonnegative.

An empirical probability distribution is uniform on a point cloud,

α=1n∑i=1nδxi.\al=\frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n\delta_{x_i}.α=n1​i=1∑n​δxi​​.
(3)

In applications, it is useful to manipulate both the positions xix_ixi​ and the weights aia_iai​. Moving the positions is a Lagrangian discretization; changing the weights is an Eulerian one. The Lagrangian view is often more adaptive, but it tends to break convexity.

General Measures¶

We consider Borel measures α∈M(X)\al\in\Mm(\X)α∈M(X) on a metric space (X,d)(\Xx,d)(X,d). This means that α(A)\al(A)α(A) is defined for every Borel set AAA, obtained from open sets by countable unions, intersections and complements. Unless otherwise stated, the measures are finite.

A Dirac measure is defined by δx(A)=1\delta_x(A)=1δx​(A)=1 if x∈Ax\in Ax∈A and 0 otherwise. For the discrete measure above,

α(A)=∑xi∈Aai.\al(A)=\sum_{x_i\in A} a_i .α(A)=xi​∈A∑​ai​.
(4)

We denote by M+(X)\Mm_+(\X)M+​(X) the set of positive finite measures on X\XX and by M+1(X)\Mm_+^1(\X)M+1​(X) the set of probability measures, i.e. positive measures of total mass one.

Definition: Support Of A Measure

The support supp⁡(α)\supp(\al)supp(α) of a Borel measure α\alα on a metric space X\XxX is the smallest closed set of full α\alα-mass. Equivalently, x∈supp⁡(α)x\in\supp(\al)x∈supp(α) if every open ball centered at xxx has positive α\alα-mass.

Radon Measures¶

Using Lebesgue integration, a Borel measure computes integrals of measurable functions. We write the duality pairing as

⟨f,α⟩:=∫f(x) dα(x).\langle f,\al\rangle \eqdef \int f(x)\d\al(x).⟨f,α⟩:=∫f(x)dα(x).
(5)

For a discrete measure this becomes

∫Xf(x) dα(x)=∑i=1naif(xi).\int_\X f(x)\d\al(x)=\sum_{i=1}^n a_i f(x_i).∫X​f(x)dα(x)=i=1∑n​ai​f(xi​).
(6)

Integration against a finite measure on a compact space defines a continuous linear form on the Banach space (C(X),∥⋅∥∞)(\Cc(\Xx),\|\cdot\|_\infty)(C(X),∥⋅∥∞​), since ∣∫f dα∣≤∥f∥∞∣α∣(X)|\int f\d\al|\leq \|f\|_\infty |\al|(\Xx)∣∫fdα∣≤∥f∥∞​∣α∣(X). Conversely, the Riesz--Markov--Kakutani representation theorem identifies every continuous linear form on C(X)\Cc(\Xx)C(X) with integration against a finite signed Radon measure Rudin, 1987Bogachev, 2007. This is the duality M(X)=C(X)∗\Mm(\Xx)=\Cc(\Xx)^*M(X)=C(X)∗ that later supports convex duality.

Relative Densities¶

A measure α\alα that is a weighting of a reference measure λ\lambdaλ has a density, denoted

 dα(x)=ρα(x) dλ(x),ρα= dα dλ.\d\al(x)=\density{\al}(x)\d\lambda(x), \qquad \density{\al}=\frac{\d\al}{\d\lambda}.dα(x)=ρα​(x)dλ(x),ρα​=dλdα​.
(7)

On Rd\RR^dRd the reference λ\lambdaλ is often Lebesgue measure  dx\d xdx. The density identity means that, for all continuous test functions hhh,

∫Xh(x) dα(x)=∫Xh(x)ρα(x) dλ(x).\int_{\Xx} h(x)\d\al(x) = \int_{\Xx} h(x)\density{\al}(x)\d\lambda(x).∫X​h(x)dα(x)=∫X​h(x)ρα​(x)dλ(x).
(8)

Total Variation¶

The norm inherited from the duality M(X)=C(X)∗\Mm(\Xx)=\Cc(\Xx)^*M(X)=C(X)∗ is the total variation norm.

Definition: Total Variation

For a finite signed Radon measure α\alα on a compact space X\XxX,

∥α∥TV:=sup⁡∥f∥∞≤1⟨f,α⟩.\|\al\|_{\TV} \eqdef \sup_{\|f\|_\infty\leq 1}\langle f,\al\rangle .∥α∥TV​:=∥f∥∞​≤1sup​⟨f,α⟩.
(9)

The absolute value of a signed measure is

∣α∣(A):=sup⁡A=∪iBi∑i∣α(Bi)∣,|\al|(A) \eqdef \sup_{A=\cup_i B_i}\sum_i |\al(B_i)|,∣α∣(A):=A=∪i​Bi​sup​i∑​∣α(Bi​)∣,
(10)

where the supremum is over finite or countable measurable partitions of AAA. If α=∑iaiδxi\al=\sum_i a_i\delta_{x_i}α=∑i​ai​δxi​​ with distinct atoms, then ∣α∣=∑i∣ai∣δxi|\al|=\sum_i |a_i|\delta_{x_i}∣α∣=∑i​∣ai​∣δxi​​. If  dα(x)=ρ(x) dλ(x)\d\al(x)=\rho(x)\d\lambda(x)dα(x)=ρ(x)dλ(x), then  d∣α∣(x)=∣ρ(x)∣ dλ(x)\d|\al|(x)=|\rho(x)|\d\lambda(x)d∣α∣(x)=∣ρ(x)∣dλ(x).

Proposition: Dual And Measure Definitions Of Total Variation

For a finite signed Radon measure α\alα on a compact space X\XxX,

∥α∥TV=∣α∣(X).\|\al\|_{\TV}=|\al|(\Xx).∥α∥TV​=∣α∣(X).
(11)

Consequently (M(X),∥⋅∥TV)(\Mm(\Xx),\|\cdot\|_{\TV})(M(X),∥⋅∥TV​) is isometrically the Banach dual of (C(X),∥⋅∥∞)(\Cc(\Xx),\|\cdot\|_\infty)(C(X),∥⋅∥∞​).

Proof

The inequality ∥α∥TV≤∣α∣(X)\|\al\|_{\TV}\leq|\al|(\Xx)∥α∥TV​≤∣α∣(X) follows from

∣∫f dα∣≤∫∣f∣ d∣α∣≤∥f∥∞∣α∣(X).\left|\int f\d\al\right| \leq \int |f|\d|\al| \leq \|f\|_\infty |\al|(\Xx).∣∣​∫fdα∣∣​≤∫∣f∣d∣α∣≤∥f∥∞​∣α∣(X).
(12)

For the reverse inequality, write the Jordan decomposition α=α+−α−\al=\al^+-\al^-α=α+−α−. The measurable sign s= dα/ d∣α∣s=\d\al/\d|\al|s=dα/d∣α∣ takes values in {−1,1}\{-1,1\}{−1,1} outside a null set and satisfies  dα=s d∣α∣\d\al=s\d|\al|dα=sd∣α∣. By regularity of Radon measures on compact spaces, sss can be approximated in L1(∣α∣)L^1(|\al|)L1(∣α∣) by continuous functions fkf_kfk​ with ∥fk∥∞≤1\|f_k\|_\infty\leq1∥fk​∥∞​≤1. Hence ∫fk dα→∫s dα=∣α∣(X)\int f_k\d\al\to\int s\d\al=|\al|(\Xx)∫fk​dα→∫sdα=∣α∣(X).

For absolutely continuous measures  dα=ρα dλ\d\al=\rho_\al\d\lambdadα=ρα​dλ and  dβ=ρβ dλ\d\be=\rho_\be\d\lambdadβ=ρβ​dλ,

∥α−β∥TV=∫X∣ρα(x)−ρβ(x)∣ dλ(x).\|\al-\be\|_{\TV} = \int_\Xx |\rho_\al(x)-\rho_\be(x)|\d\lambda(x).∥α−β∥TV​=∫X​∣ρα​(x)−ρβ​(x)∣dλ(x).
(13)

For two discrete measures written on the same union of supports, α=∑kakδzk\al=\sum_k a_k\delta_{z_k}α=∑k​ak​δzk​​ and β=∑kbkδzk\be=\sum_k b_k\delta_{z_k}β=∑k​bk​δzk​​, with missing coefficients set to zero,

∥α−β∥TV=∑k∣ak−bk∣.\|\al-\be\|_{\TV}=\sum_k |a_k-b_k|.∥α−β∥TV​=k∑​∣ak​−bk​∣.
(14)

Probabilistic Interpretation¶

Radon probability measures represent laws of random variables. A random variable with values in X\XX is a measurable map X:Ω→XX:\Omega\to\XX:Ω→X from an abstract probability space (Ω,P)(\Omega,\PP)(Ω,P). Its law is the Radon probability measure α\alα defined by

α(A)=P{ω∈Ω:X(ω)∈A}.\al(A)=\PP\{\omega\in\Omega : X(\omega)\in A\}.α(A)=P{ω∈Ω:X(ω)∈A}.
(15)

Integrals with respect to the law are expectations:

∫Xf(x) dα(x)=E[f(X)].\int_\X f(x)\d\al(x)=\mathbb{E}[f(X)].∫X​f(x)dα(x)=E[f(X)].
(16)

Push Forward¶

Push-forwards encode how maps move mass. This short section is the bridge between deterministic maps and linear operations on measures.

For a continuous map T:X→Y\T:\X\to\YT:X→Y, the push-forward operator T♯:M(X)→M(Y)\T_\sharp:\Mm(\X)\to\Mm(\Y)T♯​:M(X)→M(Y) sends a Dirac mass to T♯δx=δT(x)\T_\sharp\delta_x=\delta_{\T(x)}T♯​δx​=δT(x)​. For discrete measures,

T♯α=∑iaiδT(xi).\T_\sharp\al=\sum_i a_i\delta_{\T(x_i)}.T♯​α=i∑​ai​δT(xi​)​.
(17)

Moving from T\TT to T♯\T_\sharpT♯​ linearizes the action of a map at the price of moving from the space X\XxX to the measure space M(X)\Mm(\Xx)M(X).

Definition: Push-Forward

For T:X→Y\T:\X\to\YT:X→Y, the push-forward measure β=T♯α∈M(Y)\be=\T_\sharp\al\in\Mm(\Y)β=T♯​α∈M(Y) satisfies

∀h∈C(Y),∫Yh(y) dβ(y)=∫Xh(T(x)) dα(x).\forall h\in\Cc(\Y),\qquad \int_\Y h(y)\d\be(y) = \int_\X h(\T(x))\d\al(x).∀h∈C(Y),∫Y​h(y)dβ(y)=∫X​h(T(x))dα(x).
(18)

Equivalently, for any measurable set B⊂YB\subset\YB⊂Y,

β(B)=α({x∈X:T(x)∈B}).\be(B)=\al(\{x\in\X : \T(x)\in B\}).β(B)=α({x∈X:T(x)∈B}).
(19)

The operator T♯\T_\sharpT♯​ preserves positivity and total mass.

Remark: Pullback and push-forward

If T:X→Y\T:\X\to\YT:X→Y is continuous, the pullback by T\TT is the linear operator

T♯:C(Y)→C(X),T♯g=g∘T.\T^\sharp:\Cc(\Y)\to\Cc(\X), \qquad \T^\sharp g=g\circ\T .T♯:C(Y)→C(X),T♯g=g∘T.
(20)

The definition of the push-forward is exactly the dual relation between this pullback on functions and the action of T♯\T_\sharpT♯​ on measures:

∫XT♯g(x) dμ(x)=∫Yg(y) d(T♯μ)(y).\int_\X \T^\sharp g(x)\d\mu(x) = \int_\Y g(y)\d(\T_\sharp\mu)(y).∫X​T♯g(x)dμ(x)=∫Y​g(y)d(T♯​μ)(y).
(21)

In pairing notation,

⟨T♯g,μ⟩C(X),M(X)=⟨g,T♯μ⟩C(Y),M(Y).\left\langle \T^\sharp g,\mu\right\rangle_{\Cc(\X),\Mm(\X)} = \left\langle g,\T_\sharp\mu\right\rangle_{\Cc(\Y),\Mm(\Y)}.⟨T♯g,μ⟩C(X),M(X)​=⟨g,T♯​μ⟩C(Y),M(Y)​.
(22)

Thus push-forward is the adjoint operation to pullback, with the direction reversed. The two arrows should not be confused: T♯\T_\sharpT♯​ transports mass from X\XX to Y\YY, whereas T♯\T^\sharpT♯ transports test functions from Y\YY back to X\XX.

Proposition: Push-Forward Formula For Densities

Let α\alα and β\beβ have densities ρα\density{\al}ρα​ and ρβ\density{\be}ρβ​ on open subsets of Rd\RR^dRd. Assume that T\TT is a C1C^1C1 diffeomorphism and β=T♯α\be=\T_\sharp\alβ=T♯​α. Then

ρα(x)=∣det⁡(T′(x))∣ρβ(T(x)).\density{\al}(x) = |\det(\T'(x))|\density{\be}(\T(x)).ρα​(x)=∣det(T′(x))∣ρβ​(T(x)).
(23)

Equivalently, for y=T(x)y=\T(x)y=T(x),

ρβ(y)=ρα(T−1y)1∣det⁡(T′(T−1y))∣.\rho_\beta(y) = \rho_\alpha(\T^{-1}y) \frac{1}{|\det(\T'(\T^{-1}y))|}.ρβ​(y)=ρα​(T−1y)∣det(T′(T−1y))∣1​.
(24)
Proof

Using the change of variables y=T(x)y=\T(x)y=T(x) in the push-forward identity gives, for all h∈C(Y)h\in\Cc(\Yy)h∈C(Y),

∫Yh(y)ρβ(y) dy=∫Xh(T(x))ρα(x) dx=∫Yh(y)ρα(T−1y) dy∣det⁡(T′(T−1y))∣.\begin{aligned} \int_\Yy h(y)\rho_\be(y)\d y &= \int_\Xx h(\T(x))\rho_\al(x)\d x \\ &= \int_\Yy h(y)\rho_\al(\T^{-1}y) \frac{\d y}{|\det(\T'(\T^{-1}y))|}. \end{aligned}∫Y​h(y)ρβ​(y)dy​=∫X​h(T(x))ρα​(x)dx=∫Y​h(y)ρα​(T−1y)∣det(T′(T−1y))∣dy​.​
(25)

Since this holds for all test functions, the densities agree. Rewriting with y=T(x)y=\T(x)y=T(x) gives the displayed formula.

Remark: Probabilistic interpretation

The law, i.e. probability distribution, of a random variable XXX is the push-forward of P\PPP by XXX, namely α=X♯P\al=X_\sharp\PPα=X♯​P.

Applying another push-forward β=T♯α\be = \T_\sharp\alβ=T♯​α for T:X→Y\T : \X \rightarrow \YT:X→Y, following (18), is equivalent to defining another random variable Y=T(X)Y=\T(X)Y=T(X), namely ω∈Ω↦T(X(ω))∈Y\omega \in \Om \mapsto \T(X(\omega)) \in \Yω∈Ω↦T(X(ω))∈Y. Thus β\beβ is the law of YYY.

Drawing a random sample yyy from YYY is thus simply achieved by computing y=T(x)y=\T(x)y=T(x) where xxx is drawn from XXX.

Monge’s Formulation¶

Monge’s problem asks for a deterministic map transporting one law onto another while minimizing a prescribed cost. It is geometrically direct, because every source point is assigned one destination, but analytically fragile: the feasible set is non-convex, it can be empty, and a map cannot split mass. These limitations motivate Kantorovich’s relaxation in the next chapter.

Monge Problem¶

Given α∈M+1(X)\al\in\Mm_+^1(\Xx)α∈M+1​(X), β∈M+1(Y)\be\in\Mm_+^1(\Yy)β∈M+1​(Y) and a cost c:X×Y→R+c:\Xx\times\Yy\to\RR_+c:X×Y→R+​, the Monge problem is

Mc(α,β):=inf⁡T:X→Y{∫Xc(x,T(x)) dα(x)  :  T♯α=β}.\Monge_c(\al,\be) \eqdef \inf_{\T:\Xx\to\Yy} \left\{ \int_\Xx c(x,\T(x))\d\al(x) \;:\; \T_\sharp\al=\be \right\}.Mc​(α,β):=T:X→Yinf​{∫X​c(x,T(x))dα(x):T♯​α=β}.
(26)

The constraint T♯α=β\T_\sharp\al=\beT♯​α=β means that T\TT pushes the mass of α\alα onto β\beβ.

Proposition: Empirical Monge Maps And Matchings

Assume that the source atoms x1,…,xnx_1,\ldots,x_nx1​,…,xn​ are distinct and

α=1n∑i=1nδxi,β=1n∑j=1nδyj.\al=\frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n\delta_{x_i}, \qquad \be=\frac1n\sum_{j=1}^n\delta_{y_j}.α=n1​i=1∑n​δxi​​,β=n1​j=1∑n​δyj​​.
(27)

If T♯α=β\T_\sharp\al=\beT♯​α=β, then for each distinct target value zzz in the support of β\beβ, exactly nβ({z})n\be(\{z\})nβ({z}) source atoms are mapped to zzz. In particular, if the yjy_jyj​ are distinct, then there is a permutation σ∈Perm(n)\sigma\in\Perm(n)σ∈Perm(n) such that T(xi)=yσ(i)\T(x_i)=y_{\sigma(i)}T(xi​)=yσ(i)​.

Conversely, every assignment of source atoms to target atoms with the correct masses defines a feasible Monge map on the support of α\alα, and in the distinct-target case

∫Xc(x,T(x)) dα(x)=1n∑i=1nc(xi,yσ(i)).\int_\Xx c(x,\T(x))\d\al(x) = \frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n c(x_i,y_{\sigma(i)}).∫X​c(x,T(x))dα(x)=n1​i=1∑n​c(xi​,yσ(i)​).
(28)

If source locations are repeated, they should first be merged into atoms with larger masses; such atoms cannot be split by a Monge map.

Proof

The push-forward condition implies that all images T(xi)\T(x_i)T(xi​) belong to the support of β\beβ. For any target atom zzz,

β({z})=α(T−1({z}))=1n#{i:T(xi)=z}.\be(\{z\}) = \al(\T^{-1}(\{z\})) = \frac1n\#\{i:\T(x_i)=z\}.β({z})=α(T−1({z}))=n1​#{i:T(xi​)=z}.
(29)

This proves the counting statement. If every target atom has mass 1/n1/n1/n, each target receives exactly one source atom, hence a permutation. The converse and the cost identity follow by direct substitution.

Proposition: Existence Of Transport Maps From Atomless Sources

Let α\alα and β\beβ be Borel probability measures on Polish spaces, and assume that α\alα is atomless. Then there exists a measurable map T\TT such that T♯α=β\T_\sharp\al=\beT♯​α=β.

Proof

A standard measure-isomorphism theorem identifies the atomless probability space generated by α\alα with Lebesgue measure on [0,1][0,1][0,1], modulo null sets Bogachev, 2007. It is therefore enough to construct a map from [0,1][0,1][0,1] to the target law β\beβ. Choose a Borel isomorphism from the support of β\beβ onto a Borel subset of [0,1][0,1][0,1], push β\beβ through it, and use the generalized inverse of the corresponding cumulative distribution function. Composing back gives a measurable transport map from α\alα to β\beβ.

Remark: Feasibility versus optimality

An optimal map solving (26) might fail to exist for two distinct reasons. First, the constraint set can be empty, for instance if α=δx\al=\de_xα=δx​ and β\beβ is not a single Dirac mass. Proposition Proposition: Existence Of Transport Maps From Atomless Sources shows that non-atomicity of the source removes this feasibility obstruction. Second, even when feasible maps exist, the infimum may fail to be attained because the class of maps is not closed under weak limits.

Example: A splitting obstruction

A classical example, discussed for instance in Santambrogio, 2015, takes α\alα uniform on a vertical segment and β\beβ equal to the average of the uniform measures on two parallel vertical segments placed symmetrically to the left and to the right. For the quadratic cost, the relaxed Kantorovich optimizer splits each source point into its two symmetric targets. A deterministic Monge map cannot split one point into two destinations, so minimizing sequences must oscillate between the two sides and the Monge problem has no optimizer.

Example: Semi-discrete Monge maps

The Monge formulation is not symmetric in α\alα and β\beβ. It makes sense, for instance, when α\alα has a density with respect to Lebesgue measure and β\beβ is discrete. On X=Y=Rd\Xx=\Yy=\RR^dX=Y=Rd, let β=∑jbjδyj\be=\sum_j b_j\delta_{y_j}β=∑j​bj​δyj​​ be supported on {y1,…,ym}\{y_1,\ldots,y_m\}{y1​,…,ym​}. A map T\TT such that T♯α=β\T_\sharp\al=\beT♯​α=β defines a segmentation of the space into cells

Cj:=T−1(yj),α(Cj)=bj.C_j\eqdef \T^{-1}(y_j), \qquad \al(C_j)=b_j.Cj​:=T−1(yj​),α(Cj​)=bj​.
(30)

This is the semi-discrete setting. Chapter Paragraph explains how the cells become Laguerre cells for prescribed masses and ordinary Voronoi cells when the masses are free. If one exchanges the roles of α\alα and β\beβ so that α\alα is discrete, then no valid T\TT exists in general: it is not possible to push forward a discrete measure to a measure with density.

The next figure shows a finite-dimensional instance of this deterministic viewpoint. The source and target measures are empirical color clouds in RGB space, and the map transports colors while leaving pixel positions fixed. Grayscale equalization is one-dimensional, but full palette transfer requires transporting empirical measures in a three-dimensional color space. Early methods used affine statistics or iterated one-dimensional projections Reinhard et al., 2001Pitié et al., 2005; replacing these projections by a three-dimensional OT map gives a more intrinsic palette match Rabin et al., 2011.

show_book_figure("monge-color-transfer-rgb")

Color transfer as a Monge map in RGB space, from a beach photograph to a flower photograph. The top row applies the palette map to the source image; the bottom row shows the empirical color clouds in the RGB cube. Only colors are transported here, not pixel locations.

Interactive panel. Use the interpolation, resolution, target palette, and contrast controls to replay the RGB color transport while keeping pixel locations fixed.

Monge Distance¶

When X=Y\Xx=\YyX=Y and c(x,y)=d(x,y)pc(x,y)=d(x,y)^pc(x,y)=d(x,y)p for a metric ddd, set

Eα(T):=∫Xd(x,T(x))p dα(x).\mathcal{E}_\al(\T) \eqdef \int_\Xx d(x,\T(x))^p\d\al(x).Eα​(T):=∫X​d(x,T(x))pdα(x).
(31)

The Monge value defines the directed quantity

W~p(α,β)p:=inf⁡T:X→X{Eα(T)  :  T♯α=β}.\widetilde{\Wass}_p(\al,\be)^p \eqdef \inf_{\T:\Xx\to\Xx} \left\{ \mathcal{E}_\al(\T) \;:\; \T_\sharp\al=\be \right\}.Wp​(α,β)p:=T:X→Xinf​{Eα​(T):T♯​α=β}.
(32)

If the constraint set is empty, then W~p(α,β)=+∞\widetilde{\Wass}_p(\al,\be)=+\inftyWp​(α,β)=+∞.

Proposition: Directed Monge Distance

Assume that X=Y\Xx=\YyX=Y is a metric space. The quantity W~p\widetilde{\Wass}_pWp​ is nonnegative, vanishes only on the diagonal, and satisfies the triangle inequality. It is therefore a directed extended distance: it need not be symmetric and may take the value +∞+\infty+∞.

Proof

Nonnegativity is immediate. If W~p(α,β)=0\widetilde{\Wass}_p(\al,\be)=0Wp​(α,β)=0, choose feasible maps Tk\T_kTk​ with ∫d(x,Tk(x))p dα(x)→0\int d(x,\T_k(x))^p\d\al(x)\to0∫d(x,Tk​(x))pdα(x)→0. For every bounded 1-Lipschitz function hhh,

∣∫h dβ−∫h dα∣=∣∫h(Tk(x))−h(x) dα(x)∣≤(∫d(x,Tk(x))p dα(x))1/p→0.\left|\int h\d\be-\int h\d\al\right| = \left|\int h(\T_k(x))-h(x)\d\al(x)\right| \leq \left(\int d(x,\T_k(x))^p\d\al(x)\right)^{1/p} \to0.∣∣​∫hdβ−∫hdα∣∣​=∣∣​∫h(Tk​(x))−h(x)dα(x)∣∣​≤(∫d(x,Tk​(x))pdα(x))1/p→0.
(33)

Since bounded Lipschitz functions separate probability measures on metric spaces, α=β\al=\beα=β.

For the triangle inequality, take ϵ\epsilonϵ-minimizers S♯α=γS_\sharp\al=\gaS♯​α=γ and T♯γ=βT_\sharp\ga=\beT♯​γ=β. The composition T∘ST\circ ST∘S is feasible from α\alα to β\beβ, and Minkowski’s inequality gives

W~p(α,β)≤Eα(S)1/p+Eγ(T)1/p≤W~p(α,γ)+W~p(γ,β)+2ϵ.\widetilde{\Wass}_p(\al,\be) \leq \mathcal{E}_\al(S)^{1/p} + \mathcal{E}_\ga(T)^{1/p} \leq \widetilde{\Wass}_p(\al,\ga) + \widetilde{\Wass}_p(\ga,\be) + 2\epsilon .Wp​(α,β)≤Eα​(S)1/p+Eγ​(T)1/p≤Wp​(α,γ)+Wp​(γ,β)+2ϵ.
(34)

Letting ϵ→0\epsilon\to0ϵ→0 proves the result.

The directed value W~p\widetilde{\Wass}_pWp​ is useful conceptually, but it is too rigid to be the main distance between measures: it can be infinite and asymmetric. Kantorovich’s formulation remedies both issues by replacing maps with couplings.

Existence And Uniqueness Of The Monge Map¶

This section records the main regimes where Monge’s deterministic formulation becomes well posed. Brenier’s theorem is the central result: for the squared Euclidean cost, absolute continuity of the source restores existence, uniqueness and convex-potential structure.

Brenier’s Theorem¶

Brenier’s theorem Brenier, 1987Brenier, 1991 ensures that in Rd\RR^dRd, for the quadratic cost, absolute continuity of the source is enough for Monge’s problem to have a unique solution. It also gives the decisive structural description: the optimal map is the gradient of a convex potential.

Theorem: Brenier

Let α,β∈M+1(Rd)\al,\be\in\Mm_+^1(\RR^d)α,β∈M+1​(Rd) have finite second moments, and assume that α\alα is absolutely continuous with respect to Lebesgue measure. For c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2c(x,y)=\|x-y\|^2c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2, there exists a convex function ϕ:Rd→R∪{+∞}\phi:\RR^d\to\RR\cup\{+\infty\}ϕ:Rd→R∪{+∞} such that

T=∇ϕ,T♯α=β,\T=\nabla\phi, \qquad \T_\sharp\al=\be,T=∇ϕ,T♯​α=β,
(35)

and T\TT is the unique optimal Monge map α\alα-almost everywhere. The optimal Kantorovich plan is (Id,T)♯α(\Id,\T)_\sharp\al(Id,T)♯​α.

Proof Sketch

The proof uses Kantorovich relaxation and duality, developed later in the book. For the quadratic cost, dual optimality gives potentials whose equality set is equivalent, after subtracting quadratic terms, to the Fenchel equality ϕ(x)+ϕ∗(y)=⟨x,y⟩\phi(x)+\phi^*(y)=\langle x,y\rangleϕ(x)+ϕ∗(y)=⟨x,y⟩ for a convex function ϕ\phiϕ. Therefore the support of every optimal plan lies in the graph of ∂ϕ\partial\phi∂ϕ. Since α\alα has a density and convex functions are differentiable Lebesgue-almost everywhere, ∂ϕ(x)\partial\phi(x)∂ϕ(x) is a singleton for α\alα-almost every xxx. The relaxed optimizer is therefore induced by the map T=∇ϕ\T=\nabla\phiT=∇ϕ, and uniqueness follows from concentration on the same graph.

Definition: Measures Not Charging Hypersurfaces

A Borel measure α\alα on Rd\RR^dRd does not charge hypersurfaces if α(S)=0\al(S)=0α(S)=0 for every countably (d−1)(d-1)(d−1)-rectifiable set SSS, i.e. every set that can be covered, up to a Hd−1\mathcal{H}^{d-1}Hd−1-null set, by countably many C1C^1C1 hypersurfaces.

Remark: A sharp source hypothesis

The absolute-continuity assumption in Brenier’s theorem can be weakened: for the quadratic cost, it is enough that α\alα does not charge hypersurfaces Gangbo & McCann, 1996Villani, 2009Santambrogio, 2015. The reason is that the set where a convex potential has a genuinely multi-valued subdifferential is contained in a countable union of lower-dimensional pieces. This condition is close to sharp. If the source gives positive mass to a segment or a hypersurface, the subdifferential may be multi-valued on a set of positive α\alα-mass, and the optimal relaxed plan may need to split mass, as in Example Example: A splitting obstruction.

Remark: Beyond the quadratic Euclidean cost

Brenier’s theorem is the cleanest statement because the squared Euclidean cost turns optimal maps into gradients of convex functions. For costs c(x,y)=∥x−y∥pc(x,y)=\norm{x-y}^pc(x,y)=∥x−y∥p with p>1p>1p>1, or more generally costs c(x,y)=h(x−y)c(x,y)=h(x-y)c(x,y)=h(x−y) with hhh smooth and strictly convex, the same strategy gives a unique optimal map under absolute continuity of the source, but the map is written in terms of a ccc-convex potential. On a Riemannian manifold, the local analogue for the cost dM(x,y)2/2d_M(x,y)^2/2dM​(x,y)2/2 uses the exponential map

T(x)=exp⁡x(−∇ϕ(x)),\T(x)=\exp_x(-\nabla\phi(x)),T(x)=expx​(−∇ϕ(x)),
(36)

where ϕ\phiϕ is ccc-convex. The main additional issues are the cut locus and regularity of geodesics, which is why the Euclidean statement is usually presented first. These extensions are part of McCann’s displacement convexity theory and the general theory of optimal maps on manifolds McCann, 1997Villani, 2009.

Brenier’s theorem should be read through the analogy between convex gradients and increasing functions. The gradient of a convex function is a monotone field:

⟨∇ϕ(x)−∇ϕ(x′),x−x′⟩≥0.\langle \nabla\phi(x)-\nabla\phi(x'),x-x'\rangle\geq0.⟨∇ϕ(x)−∇ϕ(x′),x−x′⟩≥0.
(37)
Remark: Monotone fields need not be gradients

In dimensions larger than one, not all monotone fields are gradients of convex functions. Consider in R2\RR^2R2 the rotation matrix

Rθ=(cos⁡θ−sin⁡θsin⁡θcos⁡θ).R_\theta=\begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta\\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix}.Rθ​=(cosθsinθ​−sinθcosθ​).
(38)

The linear map x↦Rθxx\mapsto R_\theta xx↦Rθ​x is monotone as soon as ∣θ∣≤π/2|\theta|\leq\pi/2∣θ∣≤π/2, because

⟨Rθx−Rθx′,x−x′⟩=⟨Rθ(x−x′),x−x′⟩=cos⁡(θ)∥x−x′∥2≥0.\dotp{R_\theta x-R_\theta x'}{x-x'} = \dotp{R_\theta(x-x')}{x-x'} = \cos(\theta)\norm{x-x'}^2\geq0.⟨Rθ​x−Rθ​x′,x−x′⟩=⟨Rθ​(x−x′),x−x′⟩=cos(θ)∥x−x′∥2≥0.
(39)

However, for θ≠0\theta\neq0θ=0, RθR_\thetaRθ​ is not symmetric and therefore cannot be the gradient of a scalar potential. Indeed, if a linear field AxAxAx equals ∇ϕ(x)\nabla\phi(x)∇ϕ(x), then its Jacobian AAA must be symmetric; equivalently, a quadratic potential ϕ(x)=⟨Bx,x⟩/2\phi(x)=\dotp{Bx}{x}/2ϕ(x)=⟨Bx,x⟩/2 has gradient ((B+B⊤)/2)x((B+B^\top)/2)x((B+B⊤)/2)x. Thus monotonicity is weaker than Brenier optimality in dimension d≥2d\geq2d≥2.

Polar Factorization¶

Brenier’s theorem provides a canonical way to extract the monotone part of an arbitrary map. Suppose one starts from a square-integrable deformation u:Ω→Rdu:\Omega\to\RR^du:Ω→Rd. Its law ν=u♯λ\nu=u_\sharp\lambdaν=u♯​λ records where the mass ends up, but forgets how the points of Ω\OmegaΩ were labelled. Brenier’s polar factorization Brenier, 1987Brenier, 1991 separates these effects: a measure-preserving rearrangement sss changes labels without changing mass, then the unique convex-gradient map ∇ϕ\nabla\phi∇ϕ sends the uniform source to the output law.

Proposition: Polar Factorization

Let Ω⊂Rd\Omega\subset\RR^dΩ⊂Rd be endowed with normalized Lebesgue measure λ\lambdaλ, and let u∈L2(Ω;Rd)u\in L^2(\Omega;\RR^d)u∈L2(Ω;Rd). Assume that the law ν=u♯λ\nu=u_\sharp\lambdaν=u♯​λ has finite second moment. Then there exist a measure-preserving map s:Ω→Ωs:\Omega\to\Omegas:Ω→Ω and a convex function ϕ\phiϕ such that

u=∇ϕ∘sλ-a.e.u=\nabla\phi\circ s \qquad \lambda\text{-a.e.}u=∇ϕ∘sλ-a.e.
(40)

The Brenier factor ∇ϕ\nabla\phi∇ϕ is unique λ\lambdaλ-almost everywhere.

Proof

By Brenier’s theorem there is a unique gradient of a convex function T=∇ϕ\T=\nabla\phiT=∇ϕ transporting λ\lambdaλ to ν\nuν. The maps uuu and T\TT have the same image law. The rearrangement theorem for non-atomic probability spaces gives a measure-preserving map sss such that u=T∘su=\T\circ su=T∘s. Uniqueness of the Brenier factor follows from Brenier’s theorem.

For linear maps under a Gaussian reference, this reduces to the usual matrix polar decomposition. If X∼N(0,Id)X\sim\Gaussian(0,\Id)X∼N(0,Id) and u(x)=Axu(x)=Axu(x)=Ax, then u♯N(0,Id)=N(0,AA⊤)u_\sharp\Gaussian(0,\Id)=\Gaussian(0,AA^\top)u♯​N(0,Id)=N(0,AA⊤). The Brenier map from N(0,Id)\Gaussian(0,\Id)N(0,Id) to this Gaussian is x↦Sxx\mapsto Sxx↦Sx, where S=(AA⊤)1/2S=(AA^\top)^{1/2}S=(AA⊤)1/2 is symmetric positive semidefinite. Hence

A=SO,O=S†A.A=SO, \qquad O=S^\dagger A.A=SO,O=S†A.
(41)

The factor SxSxSx is the convex-gradient transport part, while OxOxOx preserves the standard Gaussian law.

Displacement Interpolation¶

An optimal map does not only match two endpoint measures; it tells how to draw a path between them. Each particle keeps its identity and travels at constant speed from its initial position to its image:

Tt(x):=(1−t)x+tT(x),αt:=(Tt)♯α,t∈[0,1].\T_t(x)\eqdef(1-t)x+t\T(x), \qquad \al_t\eqdef(\T_t)_\sharp\al, \qquad t\in[0,1].Tt​(x):=(1−t)x+tT(x),αt​:=(Tt​)♯​α,t∈[0,1].
(42)

For the quadratic cost, when T\TT is the Brenier map, this is McCann’s displacement interpolation McCann, 1997.

Proposition: Directed Monge Displacement Geodesics

Let p≥1p\geq1p≥1, let α,β∈M+1(Rd)\al,\be\in\Mm_+^1(\RR^d)α,β∈M+1​(Rd) have finite ppp-th moments, and let T\TT be an optimal map for W~p(α,β)\widetilde{\Wass}_p(\al,\be)Wp​(α,β). Set αt=(Tt)♯α\al_t=(\T_t)_\sharp\alαt​=(Tt​)♯​α with Tt=(1−t)Id+tT\T_t=(1-t)\Id+t\TTt​=(1−t)Id+tT. Assume that, for every t<1t<1t<1, Tt\T_tTt​ is one-to-one on a full α\alα-measure Borel set. Then, for 0≤s≤t≤10\leq s\leq t\leq10≤s≤t≤1,

W~p(αs,αt)=(t−s)W~p(α,β).\widetilde{\Wass}_p(\al_s,\al_t) = (t-s)\widetilde{\Wass}_p(\al,\be).Wp​(αs​,αt​)=(t−s)Wp​(α,β).
(43)

Thus t↦αtt\mapsto\al_tt↦αt​ is an oriented constant-speed geodesic for the directed Monge distance. For p=2p=2p=2, this applies to the Brenier map under the hypotheses of Brenier’s theorem.

Proof

For s<ts<ts<t, define Ss,t=Tt∘Ts−1S_{s,t}=\T_t\circ\T_s^{-1}Ss,t​=Tt​∘Ts−1​ along transported particles. Then (Ss,t)♯αs=αt(S_{s,t})_\sharp\al_s=\al_t(Ss,t​)♯​αs​=αt​ and

W~p(αs,αt)p≤∫∥Tt(x)−Ts(x)∥p dα(x)=(t−s)pW~p(α,β)p.\widetilde{\Wass}_p(\al_s,\al_t)^p \leq \int \|\T_t(x)-\T_s(x)\|^p\d\al(x) = (t-s)^p\widetilde{\Wass}_p(\al,\be)^p.Wp​(αs​,αt​)p≤∫∥Tt​(x)−Ts​(x)∥pdα(x)=(t−s)pWp​(α,β)p.
(44)

The reverse inequality follows by applying the triangle inequality to the three legs α→αs→αt→β\al\to\al_s\to\al_t\to\beα→αs​→αt​→β. For a Brenier map T=∇ϕ\T=\nabla\phiT=∇ϕ, Tt\T_tTt​ is the gradient of (1−t)∥x∥2/2+tϕ(x)(1-t)\|x\|^2/2+t\phi(x)(1−t)∥x∥2/2+tϕ(x), which is strongly convex for every t<1t<1t<1 and hence injective on the differentiability set of ϕ\phiϕ.

show_book_figure("monge-shape-mccann-interpolation")

McCann displacement interpolation between a cat silhouette and a heart silhouette. The first row displays a small farthest-point subset of transported particles along Tt(x)=(1−t)x+tT(x)T_t(x)=(1-t)x+tT(x)Tt​(x)=(1−t)x+tT(x). The second row renders kernel-smoothed densities from a denser transported cloud as color images: white means zero density, while high density saturates in the red-to-blue interpolation color of the corresponding time.

Interactive panel. Use the interpolation and particle controls to compare the particle motion with the evolving density during McCann displacement interpolation.

Regularity And The Monge-Ampere Equation¶

The previous results identify the optimal map. Regularity theory asks when this map is a classical smooth deformation rather than only an almost-everywhere gradient. For quadratic costs this becomes the regularity theory of the Monge--Ampere equation.

Proposition: Caffarelli Regularity

Let Ω,Λ⊂Rd\Omega,\Lambda\subset\RR^dΩ,Λ⊂Rd be bounded uniformly convex domains with C2C^2C2 boundaries. Let α=ρ(x) dx\al=\rho(x)\d xα=ρ(x)dx be supported on Ω\OmegaΩ and β=η(y) dy\be=\eta(y)\d yβ=η(y)dy be supported on Λ\LambdaΛ, with 0<m≤ρ,η≤M<+∞0<m\leq\rho,\eta\leq M<+\infty0<m≤ρ,η≤M<+∞. If ρ,η∈Cα\rho,\eta\in C^\alphaρ,η∈Cα for some α∈(0,1)\alpha\in(0,1)α∈(0,1), then the Brenier potential ϕ\phiϕ transporting α\alα to β\beβ is strictly convex and satisfies ϕ∈Cloc2,α(Ω)\phi\in C^{2,\alpha}_{\mathrm{loc}}(\Omega)ϕ∈Cloc2,α​(Ω). Under corresponding boundary compatibility and smoothness assumptions, the regularity holds up to the boundary.

Proof Sketch

The potential solves the Monge--Ampere equation

det⁡(∇2ϕ(x))=ρ(x)η(∇ϕ(x))\det(\nabla^2\phi(x)) = \frac{\rho(x)}{\eta(\nabla\phi(x))}det(∇2ϕ(x))=η(∇ϕ(x))ρ(x)​
(45)

in the Alexandrov sense, with second boundary condition ∇ϕ(Ω)=Λ\nabla\phi(\Omega)=\Lambda∇ϕ(Ω)=Λ. Density bounds and convexity of the domains give strict convexity and localization of sections. Caffarelli’s interior theory then yields the Cloc2,αC^{2,\alpha}_{\mathrm{loc}}Cloc2,α​ estimates Caffarelli, 2003Villani, 2009.

Remark: Regularity, weak maps, and splitting

Caffarelli’s theorem should be read as a warning as well as a theorem. Brenier’s theorem gives existence and uniqueness under mild assumptions, but smoothness requires density bounds, smoothness and convex geometry that are rarely satisfied by empirical, manifold-supported or neural generative distributions. In such applications, the exact OT map is often only weakly defined, possibly unstable, and better represented by a coupling, an entropic approximation or a learned parametric surrogate.

Even without smoothness, the convex potential is locally Lipschitz on the interior of its domain, so ∇ϕ\nabla\phi∇ϕ is defined Lebesgue-almost everywhere. If the source measure does not satisfy the non-splitting hypotheses of Brenier’s theorem, the correct relaxed object is instead an optimal Kantorovich plan concentrated on the graph of the set-valued map ∂ϕ\partial\phi∂ϕ. At points where ∂ϕ(x)\partial\phi(x)∂ϕ(x) contains several target locations, the plan may split the mass starting from xxx. Thus the subdifferential still describes the geometry of optimality, but the transport object is a coupling rather than a single-valued map.

For smooth densities, the change-of-variables formula gives the Monge--Ampere equation

det⁡(∇2ϕ(x))ρβ(∇ϕ(x))=ρα(x).\det(\nabla^2\phi(x))\density{\be}(\nabla\phi(x)) = \density{\al}(x).det(∇2ϕ(x))ρβ​(∇ϕ(x))=ρα​(x).
(46)

With suitable boundary conditions, this characterizes the Brenier potential up to an additive constant among convex solutions. The following proposition records the infinitesimal form.

Proposition: Linearization Of The Monge-Ampere Equation

Let ρϵ=ρ0+ϵr+o(ϵ)\rho_\epsilon=\rho_0+\epsilon r+o(\epsilon)ρϵ​=ρ0​+ϵr+o(ϵ) be a smooth perturbation of a positive reference density ρ0\rho_0ρ0​ on a smooth bounded domain, with ∫r=0\int r=0∫r=0. If Tϵ(x)=x+ϵ∇u(x)+o(ϵ)\T_\epsilon(x)=x+\epsilon\nabla u(x)+o(\epsilon)Tϵ​(x)=x+ϵ∇u(x)+o(ϵ) transports ρ0\rho_0ρ0​ to ρϵ\rho_\epsilonρϵ​, then, to first order,

−∇⋅(ρ0∇u)=r.-\nabla\cdot(\rho_0\nabla u)=r.−∇⋅(ρ0​∇u)=r.
(47)

When ρ0\rho_0ρ0​ is constant, the linearized equation is −Δu=r/ρ0-\Delta u=r/\rho_0−Δu=r/ρ0​.

Proof

The change-of-variables equation for Tϵ\T_\epsilonTϵ​ is

ρ0(x)=ρϵ(Tϵ(x))det⁡(∇Tϵ(x)).\rho_0(x) = \rho_\epsilon(\T_\epsilon(x))\det(\nabla\T_\epsilon(x)).ρ0​(x)=ρϵ​(Tϵ​(x))det(∇Tϵ​(x)).
(48)

Expanding ρϵ(x+ϵ∇u)=ρ0(x)+ϵr(x)+ϵ⟨∇ρ0,∇u⟩+o(ϵ)\rho_\epsilon(x+\epsilon\nabla u)=\rho_0(x)+\epsilon r(x)+ \epsilon\langle\nabla\rho_0,\nabla u\rangle+o(\epsilon)ρϵ​(x+ϵ∇u)=ρ0​(x)+ϵr(x)+ϵ⟨∇ρ0​,∇u⟩+o(ϵ) and

det⁡(I+ϵ∇2u)=1+ϵΔu+o(ϵ)\det(I+\epsilon\nabla^2u)=1+\epsilon\Delta u+o(\epsilon)det(I+ϵ∇2u)=1+ϵΔu+o(ϵ)
(49)

gives

ρ0=ρ0+ϵ(r+∇⋅(ρ0∇u))+o(ϵ).\rho_0 = \rho_0 + \epsilon\left(r+\nabla\cdot(\rho_0\nabla u)\right) + o(\epsilon).ρ0​=ρ0​+ϵ(r+∇⋅(ρ0​∇u))+o(ϵ).
(50)

The first-order term must vanish.

One-Dimensional Transport And Quantiles¶

In one dimension, optimal transport is completely explicit. The cumulative distribution function orders the mass, and the optimal coupling is obtained by matching equal quantile levels. This case is both a computational tool and the template for several linearized constructions used later.

Definition: Cumulative And Quantile Functions

For α∈M+1(R)\al\in\Mm_+^1(\RR)α∈M+1​(R), its cumulative distribution function is

Fα(x):=α((−∞,x]).\cumul{\al}(x)\eqdef \al((-\infty,x]).Fα​(x):=α((−∞,x]).
(51)

Its generalized inverse, or quantile function, is

Fα−1(r):=inf⁡{x∈R:Fα(x)≥r},r∈(0,1).\cumul{\al}^{-1}(r) \eqdef \inf\{x\in\RR:\cumul{\al}(x)\geq r\}, \qquad r\in(0,1).Fα−1​(r):=inf{x∈R:Fα​(x)≥r},r∈(0,1).
(52)
Proposition: Quantile Push-Forward

One has (Fα−1)♯Leb[0,1]=α(\cumul{\al}^{-1})_\sharp\mathrm{Leb}_{[0,1]}=\al(Fα−1​)♯​Leb[0,1]​=α. If α\alα has no atoms, then (Fα)♯α=Leb[0,1](\cumul{\al})_\sharp\al=\mathrm{Leb}_{[0,1]}(Fα​)♯​α=Leb[0,1]​.

Proof

Assume first that α\alα has a strictly positive density, so that Fα\cumul{\al}Fα​ is strictly increasing and continuous. Let γ=(Fα−1)♯Leb[0,1]\ga=(\cumul{\al}^{-1})_\sharp\mathrm{Leb}_{[0,1]}γ=(Fα−1​)♯​Leb[0,1]​. For every xxx,

Fγ(x)=∫011(−∞,x](Fα−1(z)) dz=∫011[0,Fα(x)](z) dz=Fα(x).\cumul{\ga}(x) = \int_0^1 \mathbf{1}_{(-\infty,x]}(\cumul{\al}^{-1}(z))\d z = \int_0^1 \mathbf{1}_{[0,\cumul{\al}(x)]}(z)\d z = \cumul{\al}(x).Fγ​(x)=∫01​1(−∞,x]​(Fα−1​(z))dz=∫01​1[0,Fα​(x)]​(z)dz=Fα​(x).
(53)

General measures follow from the same argument with generalized inverses and right-continuity. If α\alα has no atoms, the probability integral transform gives (Fα)♯α=Leb[0,1](\cumul{\al})_\sharp\al=\mathrm{Leb}_{[0,1]}(Fα​)♯​α=Leb[0,1]​.

If α\alα has no atoms, the map

T=Fβ−1∘Fα\T=\cumul{\be}^{-1}\circ\cumul{\al}T=Fβ−1​∘Fα​
(54)

satisfies T♯α=β\T_\sharp\al=\beT♯​α=β. For the cost c(x,y)=∣x−y∣2c(x,y)=|x-y|^2c(x,y)=∣x−y∣2, this map is nondecreasing and therefore the derivative of a convex function in dimension one.

Proposition: Monotone Rearrangement On The Line

Let α,β∈M+1(R)\al,\be\in\Mm_+^1(\RR)α,β∈M+1​(R) have finite ppp-th moments, with p≥1p\geq1p≥1. The coupling

π⋆=(Fα−1,Fβ−1)♯Leb[0,1]\pi^\star = (\cumul{\al}^{-1},\cumul{\be}^{-1})_\sharp\mathrm{Leb}_{[0,1]}π⋆=(Fα−1​,Fβ−1​)♯​Leb[0,1]​
(55)

minimizes ∫∣x−y∣p dπ(x,y)\int |x-y|^p\d\pi(x,y)∫∣x−y∣pdπ(x,y) among couplings. If α\alα has no atoms, it is induced by the monotone Monge map above.

Proof

The displayed measure is a coupling by the quantile push-forward proposition. Its support is monotone: equal quantile levels cannot create crossing pairs. If a coupling had two crossed pairs x<x′x<x'x<x′ and y>y′y>y'y>y′ with positive mass, exchanging the targets decreases the cost for strictly convex powers and does not increase it for p=1p=1p=1, by the two-point inequality from the matching chapter. Eliminating crossings gives the quantile coupling. If α\alα has no atoms, the map formula follows from (Fα)♯α=Leb[0,1](\cumul{\al})_\sharp\al=\mathrm{Leb}_{[0,1]}(Fα​)♯​α=Leb[0,1]​.

Remark: Composition is one-dimensional

In dimension one, optimal maps compose. Assume for simplicity that the intermediate laws have no atoms, so that the monotone rearrangements

Tα→β=Fβ−1∘Fα,Tβ→γ=Fγ−1∘Fβ\T_{\al\to\be}=\cumul{\be}^{-1}\circ\cumul{\al}, \qquad \T_{\be\to\ga}=\cumul{\ga}^{-1}\circ\cumul{\be}Tα→β​=Fβ−1​∘Fα​,Tβ→γ​=Fγ−1​∘Fβ​
(56)

are well defined α\alα- and β\beβ-almost everywhere. Then

Tβ→γ∘Tα→β=Tα→γα-a.e.\T_{\be\to\ga}\circ\T_{\al\to\be} = \T_{\al\to\ga} \qquad \al\text{-a.e.}Tβ→γ​∘Tα→β​=Tα→γ​α-a.e.
(57)

Indeed, each map is nondecreasing and sends quantile level rrr to the same quantile level of the target law. This semigroup property is special to the ordered line.

Proposition: One-Dimensional Wasserstein Formulas

Let α,β∈M+1(R)\al,\be\in\Mm_+^1(\RR)α,β∈M+1​(R) have finite ppp-th moments. For p≥1p\geq1p≥1,

Wp(α,β)p=∫01∣Fα−1(r)−Fβ−1(r)∣p dr=∥Fα−1−Fβ−1∥Lp([0,1])p.\Wass_p(\al,\be)^p = \int_0^1 \left|\cumul{\al}^{-1}(r)-\cumul{\be}^{-1}(r)\right|^p\d r = \|\cumul{\al}^{-1}-\cumul{\be}^{-1}\|_{L^p([0,1])}^p.Wp​(α,β)p=∫01​∣∣​Fα−1​(r)−Fβ−1​(r)∣∣​pdr=∥Fα−1​−Fβ−1​∥Lp([0,1])p​.
(58)

For p=1p=1p=1,

W1(α,β)=∥Fα−Fβ∥L1(R)=∫R∣Fα(x)−Fβ(x)∣ dx.\Wass_1(\al,\be) = \|\cumul{\al}-\cumul{\be}\|_{L^1(\RR)} = \int_\RR |\cumul{\al}(x)-\cumul{\be}(x)|\d x .W1​(α,β)=∥Fα​−Fβ​∥L1(R)​=∫R​∣Fα​(x)−Fβ​(x)∣dx.
(59)
Proof

The first formula follows because the optimal coupling is obtained by taking the same quantile level rrr for both measures. For p=1p=1p=1, use the layer-cake identity. If qαq_\alqα​ and qβq_\beqβ​ are the quantile functions, then

∫01∣qα(r)−qβ(r)∣ dr=∫Rλ{r:qα(r)≤x<qβ(r) or qβ(r)≤x<qα(r)} dx,\int_0^1 |q_\al(r)-q_\be(r)|\d r = \int_\RR \lambda\{r:q_\al(r)\leq x<q_\be(r)\ \text{or}\ q_\be(r)\leq x<q_\al(r)\} \d x,∫01​∣qα​(r)−qβ​(r)∣dr=∫R​λ{r:qα​(r)≤x<qβ​(r) or qβ​(r)≤x<qα​(r)}dx,
(60)

and the measure of the set inside the integral is ∣Fα(x)−Fβ(x)∣|\cumul{\al}(x)-\cumul{\be}(x)|∣Fα​(x)−Fβ​(x)∣ for almost every xxx.

The quantile formula above means that α↦Fα−1\al\mapsto\cumul{\al}^{-1}α↦Fα−1​ embeds one-dimensional Wasserstein geometry isometrically into a linear LpL^pLp space. For p=2p=2p=2, Wasserstein geometry on probability measures over the real line is Hilbertian.

show_book_figure("monge-1d-quantile-geodesic")

One-dimensional transport through quantiles. The same two smooth laws are shown as densities, cumulative functions and quantile functions. The last panel displays the displacement interpolation obtained by the linear quantile path Qt=(1−t)Qα+tQβQ_t=(1-t)Q_\alpha+tQ_\betaQt​=(1−t)Qα​+tQβ​, which is the explicit one-dimensional W2\Wass_2W2​ geodesic.

Interactive panel. Use the time and endpoint controls to follow the one-dimensional Wasserstein geodesic through quantiles, CDFs, and densities.

In quantile coordinates, the interpolating measure is characterized by

Fαt−1(r)=(1−t)Fα−1(r)+tFβ−1(r),r∈(0,1).\cumul{\al_t}^{-1}(r) = (1-t)\cumul{\al}^{-1}(r)+t\cumul{\be}^{-1}(r), \qquad r\in(0,1).Fαt​−1​(r)=(1−t)Fα−1​(r)+tFβ−1​(r),r∈(0,1).
(61)

For p=1p=1p=1, the cumulative formula above shows that W1\Wass_1W1​ is a norm on signed measures with zero total mass once they are identified with their cumulative primitives.

Triangular Rearrangements¶

There is another canonical way to build transport maps in several dimensions: transport one coordinate at a time by conditional one-dimensional quantiles. This construction is not usually cost-optimal, but it gives a deterministic rearrangement under weak assumptions.

Proposition: Knothe--Rosenblatt Triangular Rearrangement

Let α,β∈M+1(Rd)\al,\be\in\Mm_+^1(\RR^d)α,β∈M+1​(Rd). Assume, for simplicity, that the first marginal of α\alα and the one-dimensional conditional laws of α\alα used below are atomless. There is a triangular map

T(x1,…,xd)=(T1(x1),T2(x1,x2),…,Td(x1,…,xd))\T(x_1,\ldots,x_d) = (\T_1(x_1),\T_2(x_1,x_2),\ldots,\T_d(x_1,\ldots,x_d))T(x1​,…,xd​)=(T1​(x1​),T2​(x1​,x2​),…,Td​(x1​,…,xd​))
(62)

such that T♯α=β\T_\sharp\al=\beT♯​α=β and, for each kkk, the function xk↦Tk(x1,…,xk)x_k\mapsto\T_k(x_1,\ldots,x_k)xk​↦Tk​(x1​,…,xk​) is nondecreasing for α\alα-almost every value of (x1,…,xk−1)(x_1,\ldots,x_{k-1})(x1​,…,xk−1​).

Proof

The construction is recursive. For k=1k=1k=1, let T1\T_1T1​ be the monotone rearrangement between the first marginals of α\alα and β\beβ. Suppose T1,…,Tk−1\T_1,\ldots,\T_{k-1}T1​,…,Tk−1​ have been constructed. Write x<k=(x1,…,xk−1)x_{<k}=(x_1,\ldots,x_{k-1})x<k​=(x1​,…,xk−1​) and T<k=(T1,…,Tk−1)\T_{<k}=(\T_1,\ldots,\T_{k-1})T<k​=(T1​,…,Tk−1​). Let αx<kk\al^k_{x_{<k}}αx<k​k​ and βy<kk\be^k_{y_{<k}}βy<k​k​ be regular conditional laws of the kkk-th coordinate given the previous coordinates. Define Tk(x<k,⋅)\T_k(x_{<k},\cdot)Tk​(x<k​,⋅) as the one-dimensional monotone rearrangement from αx<kk\al^k_{x_{<k}}αx<k​k​ to βT<k(x<k)k\be^k_{\T_{<k}(x_{<k})}βT<k​(x<k​)k​. The chain rule for disintegrations shows that after step kkk the first kkk coordinates of T♯α\T_\sharp\alT♯​α match those of β\beβ.

show_book_figure("monge-triangular-rearrangement")

Triangular rearrangement between the same cat and heart densities as in the McCann interpolation figure. The panels are computed directly on image histograms. The first three transitions move mass horizontally by the monotone rearrangement between the xxx-marginals; the pivot has the target horizontal marginal. The last three transitions keep each column fixed and move mass vertically by one-dimensional monotone rearrangements between conditional laws.

Interactive panel. Use the horizontal and vertical interpolation sliders to inspect the Knothe triangular rearrangement one coordinate update at a time.

This construction transports successively along coordinate axes and is often called axis-wise transport. It depends on the chosen ordering of coordinates and is not generally optimal for the quadratic cost. It is nevertheless a useful limiting object: Brenier maps for increasingly anisotropic quadratic costs converge to triangular rearrangements under suitable assumptions Carlier et al., 2010.

Proposition: Anisotropic Brenier Maps Converge To Knothe--Rosenblatt

Let α,β\alpha,\betaα,β be compactly supported probability measures on Rd\RR^dRd with densities bounded above and below on rectangular supports, and assume that the conditional laws entering the triangular rearrangement are atomless. For ϵ>0\epsilon>0ϵ>0, set

cϵ(x,y):=∑k=1dϵk−1∣xk−yk∣2.c_\epsilon(x,y) \eqdef \sum_{k=1}^d \epsilon^{k-1}|x_k-y_k|^2 .cϵ​(x,y):=k=1∑d​ϵk−1∣xk​−yk​∣2.
(63)

Let TϵT_\epsilonTϵ​ be the Monge map from α\alphaα to β\betaβ for cϵc_\epsiloncϵ​, and let TKRT_{\mathrm{KR}}TKR​ be the triangular Knothe--Rosenblatt rearrangement with the same coordinate order. Then

Tϵ⟶TKRin L2(α;Rd)as ϵ→0.T_\epsilon \longrightarrow T_{\mathrm{KR}} \qquad\text{in }L^2(\alpha;\RR^d) \qquad\text{as }\epsilon\to0 .Tϵ​⟶TKR​in L2(α;Rd)as ϵ→0.
(64)
Proof Sketch

Let πϵ=(Id,Tϵ)♯α\pi_\epsilon=(\Id,T_\epsilon)_\sharp\alphaπϵ​=(Id,Tϵ​)♯​α. Compactness gives a weakly convergent subsequence. Optimality for

Fϵ(γ)=∑k=1dϵk−1∫∣xk−yk∣2 dγ(x,y)F_\epsilon(\gamma) = \sum_{k=1}^d \epsilon^{k-1} \int |x_k-y_k|^2\d\gamma(x,y)Fϵ​(γ)=k=1∑d​ϵk−1∫∣xk​−yk​∣2dγ(x,y)
(65)

first forces any weak limit to minimize the one-dimensional quadratic cost in the first coordinate, hence to realize the first monotone rearrangement. Subtract that common minimum, divide by ϵ\epsilonϵ, and let ϵ→0\epsilon\to0ϵ→0 to identify the second coordinate as a conditional monotone rearrangement. Repeating gives the triangular graph coupling. Since the graph coupling is unique, all weak limits agree. A Lusin and Portmanteau argument then upgrades convergence in law to convergence in L2(α)L^2(\alpha)L2(α) because the maps take values in a common compact set.

Example: Linear obstruction to composing Brenier maps

In higher dimension, Brenier maps for the quadratic cost are gradients of convex functions, and such maps do not generally remain gradients after composition. The simplest obstruction is linear. If T1(x)=A1x\T_1(x)=A_1xT1​(x)=A1​x and T2(x)=A2x\T_2(x)=A_2xT2​(x)=A2​x with A1,A2A_1,A_2A1​,A2​ symmetric positive definite, then T2∘T1\T_2\circ\T_1T2​∘T1​ has matrix A2A1A_2A_1A2​A1​. It is a gradient field only when this product is symmetric, equivalently A1A2=A2A1A_1A_2=A_2A_1A1​A2​=A2​A1​. Gaussian transport gives a concrete instance: between nondegenerate Gaussian laws, the Brenier map is affine with symmetric positive definite linear part. Compositions of Gaussian optimal maps are therefore optimal only in special commuting situations, for instance when all covariance matrices are simultaneously diagonalizable. Otherwise the composition contains a rotational or shearing component and is not the Brenier map between the initial and final Gaussians.

Algorithm: Quantile rearrangement and one-dimensional geodesic

Input: One-dimensional probability measures α,β\alpha,\betaα,β; time t∈[0,1]t\in[0,1]t∈[0,1].

Output: Quantile coupling, Monge map when defined, and geodesic point αt\alpha_tαt​.

Compute Fα\cumul{\al}Fα​, Fβ\cumul{\be}Fβ​ and generalized inverses.

Couple equal quantile levels: (X=\cumul{\al}^{-1}(r), \qquad Y=\cumul{\be}^{-1}(r), \qquad r\in(0,1).)

If α\alphaα has no atoms then:

Set (T(x)=\cumul{\be}^{-1}(\cumul{\al}(x)).)

Interpolate quantiles: (Q_t(r)=(1-t)\cumul{\al}^{-1}(r)+t\cumul{\be}^{-1}(r), \qquad \al_t=(Q_t)\sharp\mathrm{Leb}{[0,1]}.) Return (X,Y)(X,Y)(X,Y), TTT if defined, and αt\alpha_tαt​.

Algorithm: Knothe--Rosenblatt triangular rearrangement

Input: Probability measures α,β\alpha,\betaα,β on Rd\RR^dRd with conditional laws.

Output: Knothe--Rosenblatt triangular map T\TT.

Compute first-coordinate rearrangement: (\T_1=(F_{\be_1})^{-1}\circ F_{\al_1}.)

For k=2,…,dk=2,\ldots,dk=2,…,d do:

Set x<k=(x1,…,xk−1)x_{<k}=(x_1,\ldots,x_{k-1})x<k​=(x1​,…,xk−1​).

Compute conditional laws αx<kk\al^k_{x_{<k}}αx<k​k​ and βT<k(x<k)k\be^k_{\T_{<k}(x_{<k})}βT<k​(x<k​)k​.

Set (\T_k(x_{<k},x_k) = \bigl(F_{\be^k_{\T_{<k}(x_{<k})}}\bigr)^{-1} \circ F_{\al^k_{x_{<k}}}(x_k).)

Return (\T(x)=(\T_1(x_1),\T_2(x_1,x_2),\ldots,\T_d(x_1,\ldots,x_d)).)

Gaussian Measures And The Bures Metric¶

Gaussian measures form the most important finite-dimensional family preserved by quadratic optimal transport. The mean moves linearly, while the covariance follows the Bures--Wasserstein geometry of positive semidefinite matrices.

One-Dimensional Gaussians¶

Let α=N(mα,σα2)\al=\Gaussian(m_\al,\sigma_\al^2)α=N(mα​,σα2​) and β=N(mβ,σβ2)\be=\Gaussian(m_\be,\sigma_\be^2)β=N(mβ​,σβ2​) be nondegenerate Gaussians on R\RRR. Then

T(x)=σβσα(x−mα)+mβ\T(x)=\frac{\sigma_\be}{\sigma_\al}(x-m_\al)+m_\beT(x)=σα​σβ​​(x−mα​)+mβ​
(66)

satisfies T♯α=β\T_\sharp\al=\beT♯​α=β. It is the derivative of the convex function

ϕ(x)=σβ2σα(x−mα)2+mβx,\phi(x)=\frac{\sigma_\be}{2\sigma_\al}(x-m_\al)^2+m_\be x,ϕ(x)=2σα​σβ​​(x−mα​)2+mβ​x,
(67)

so Brenier’s theorem shows that it is the optimal quadratic transport. The distance is

W2(α,β)2=(mα−mβ)2+(σα−σβ)2.\Wass_2(\al,\be)^2 = (m_\al-m_\be)^2+(\sigma_\al-\sigma_\be)^2.W2​(α,β)2=(mα​−mβ​)2+(σα​−σβ​)2.
(68)

Thus the OT geometry of one-dimensional Gaussians is the Euclidean geometry of the half-plane (m,σ)∈R×R+(m,\sigma)\in\RR\times\RR_+(m,σ)∈R×R+​.

Multivariate Gaussians¶

If

α=N(mα,Σα),β=N(mβ,Σβ),T(x)=mβ+A(x−mα),\al=\Gaussian(\mean_\al,\cov_\al), \qquad \be=\Gaussian(\mean_\be,\cov_\be), \qquad \T(x)=\mean_\be+A(x-\mean_\al),α=N(mα​,Σα​),β=N(mβ​,Σβ​),T(x)=mβ​+A(x−mα​),
(69)

then T\TT is the gradient of a convex quadratic potential if and only if AAA is symmetric positive semidefinite.

Proposition: Affine Push-Forward Of Gaussians

One has T♯α=β\T_\sharp\al=\beT♯​α=β if and only if

AΣαA⊤=Σβ.A\cov_\al A^\top=\cov_\be .AΣα​A⊤=Σβ​.
(70)
Proof

An affine function maps a Gaussian to a Gaussian, so the law of T(X)\T(X)T(X) is determined by mean and covariance. If X∼αX\sim\alX∼α and Y=T(X)Y=\T(X)Y=T(X), then

E(Y)=mβ,E((Y−mβ)(Y−mβ)⊤)=AΣαA⊤.\mathbb{E}(Y)=\mean_\be, \qquad \mathbb{E}((Y-\mean_\be)(Y-\mean_\be)^\top) = A\cov_\al A^\top.E(Y)=mβ​,E((Y−mβ​)(Y−mβ​)⊤)=AΣα​A⊤.
(71)
Proposition: Gaussian W2\Wass_2W2​ Formula And Bures Covariance Term

Assume that Σα\cov_\alΣα​ and Σβ\cov_\beΣβ​ are positive definite. The unique symmetric positive-definite solution of AΣαA=ΣβA\cov_\al A=\cov_\beAΣα​A=Σβ​ is

A=Σα−1/2(Σα1/2ΣβΣα1/2)1/2Σα−1/2.A= \cov_\al^{-1/2} \left(\cov_\al^{1/2}\cov_\be\cov_\al^{1/2}\right)^{1/2} \cov_\al^{-1/2}.A=Σα−1/2​(Σα1/2​Σβ​Σα1/2​)1/2Σα−1/2​.
(72)

The affine map T(x)=mβ+A(x−mα)\T(x)=\mean_\be+A(x-\mean_\al)T(x)=mβ​+A(x−mα​) is the optimal quadratic-cost transport from N(mα,Σα)\Gaussian(\mean_\al,\cov_\al)N(mα​,Σα​) to N(mβ,Σβ)\Gaussian(\mean_\be,\cov_\be)N(mβ​,Σβ​), and

W2(α,β)2=∥mα−mβ∥2+B(Σα,Σβ)2,\Wass_2(\al,\be)^2 = \|\mean_\al-\mean_\be\|^2+\Bb(\cov_\al,\cov_\be)^2,W2​(α,β)2=∥mα​−mβ​∥2+B(Σα​,Σβ​)2,
(73)

where

B(Σα,Σβ)2:=tr⁡(Σα+Σβ−2(Σα1/2ΣβΣα1/2)1/2).\Bb(\cov_\al,\cov_\be)^2 \eqdef \tr\left( \cov_\al+\cov_\be -2(\cov_\al^{1/2}\cov_\be\cov_\al^{1/2})^{1/2} \right).B(Σα​,Σβ​)2:=tr(Σα​+Σβ​−2(Σα1/2​Σβ​Σα1/2​)1/2).
(74)
Proof

Multiplying AΣαA=ΣβA\cov_\al A=\cov_\beAΣα​A=Σβ​ on the left and right by Σα1/2\cov_\al^{1/2}Σα1/2​ gives

(Σα1/2AΣα1/2)2=Σα1/2ΣβΣα1/2.(\cov_\al^{1/2}A\cov_\al^{1/2})^2 = \cov_\al^{1/2}\cov_\be\cov_\al^{1/2}.(Σα1/2​AΣα1/2​)2=Σα1/2​Σβ​Σα1/2​.
(75)

The positive square root gives the displayed formula for AAA. This map pushes α\alα to β\beβ and is a gradient of a convex quadratic potential, hence is optimal by Brenier. If X∼αX\sim\alX∼α,

E∥X−T(X)∥2=∥mα−mβ∥2+tr⁡((I−A)Σα(I−A)⊤)=∥mα−mβ∥2+tr⁡(Σα)+tr⁡(Σβ)−2tr⁡((Σα1/2ΣβΣα1/2)1/2).\begin{aligned} \mathbb{E}\|X-\T(X)\|^2 &= \|\mean_\al-\mean_\be\|^2 + \tr((I-A)\cov_\al(I-A)^\top) \\ &= \|\mean_\al-\mean_\be\|^2 + \tr(\cov_\al)+\tr(\cov_\be) -2\tr((\cov_\al^{1/2}\cov_\be\cov_\al^{1/2})^{1/2}). \end{aligned}E∥X−T(X)∥2​=∥mα​−mβ​∥2+tr((I−A)Σα​(I−A)⊤)=∥mα​−mβ​∥2+tr(Σα​)+tr(Σβ​)−2tr((Σα1/2​Σβ​Σα1/2​)1/2).​
(76)

The covariance term B\BbB is the Bures--Wasserstein metric on positive semidefinite matrices Bures, 1969Gelbrich, 1990Bhatia et al., 2019. It separates Euclidean displacement of the mean from the intrinsic transport geometry of covariance ellipsoids.

show_book_figure("monge-gaussian-w2-geodesic")

One- and two-dimensional Gaussian W2\Wass_2W2​ geodesics. In one dimension, the coordinates (m,σ)(m,\sigma)(m,σ) turn geodesics into Euclidean segments in the upper half-plane. In two dimensions, means move linearly while covariance ellipses follow the Bures--Wasserstein interpolation.

Interactive panel. Use the target mean, variance, and angle controls to see how the Gaussian Wasserstein geodesic moves means and covariance ellipses.

Proposition: Metric And Convexity Properties Of The Bures Term

The function B\BbB is a distance on positive semidefinite covariance matrices. Moreover, B2\Bb^2B2 is jointly convex:

B2((1−t)Σ0+tΣ1,(1−t)Λ0+tΛ1)≤(1−t)B2(Σ0,Λ0)+tB2(Σ1,Λ1).\Bb^2((1-t)\Sigma_0+t\Sigma_1,(1-t)\Lambda_0+t\Lambda_1) \leq (1-t)\Bb^2(\Sigma_0,\Lambda_0) + t\Bb^2(\Sigma_1,\Lambda_1).B2((1−t)Σ0​+tΣ1​,(1−t)Λ0​+tΛ1​)≤(1−t)B2(Σ0​,Λ0​)+tB2(Σ1​,Λ1​).
(77)
Proof

The key identity is the Procrustes representation

B2(Σ,Λ)=min⁡Q⊤Q=I∥Σ1/2−Λ1/2Q∥F2.\Bb^2(\Sigma,\Lambda) = \min_{Q^\top Q=I}\|\Sigma^{1/2}-\Lambda^{1/2}Q\|_F^2.B2(Σ,Λ)=Q⊤Q=Imin​∥Σ1/2−Λ1/2Q∥F2​.
(78)

Symmetry, positivity and separation follow immediately. The triangle inequality follows by choosing two almost optimal orthogonal matrices and applying the usual triangle inequality for the Frobenius norm.

For convexity, use the factor formulation

B2(Σ,Λ)=min⁡UU⊤=Σ,  VV⊤=Λ∥U−V∥F2.\Bb^2(\Sigma,\Lambda) = \min_{UU^\top=\Sigma,\;VV^\top=\Lambda}\|U-V\|_F^2.B2(Σ,Λ)=UU⊤=Σ,VV⊤=Λmin​∥U−V∥F2​.
(79)

Choose nearly optimal factors (U0,V0)(U_0,V_0)(U0​,V0​) and (U1,V1)(U_1,V_1)(U1​,V1​), and set

Ut=[1−t U0,t U1],Vt=[1−t V0,t V1].U_t=[\sqrt{1-t}\,U_0,\sqrt t\,U_1], \qquad V_t=[\sqrt{1-t}\,V_0,\sqrt t\,V_1].Ut​=[1−t​U0​,t​U1​],Vt​=[1−t​V0​,t​V1​].
(80)

Then UtUt⊤=(1−t)Σ0+tΣ1U_tU_t^\top=(1-t)\Sigma_0+t\Sigma_1Ut​Ut⊤​=(1−t)Σ0​+tΣ1​ and VtVt⊤=(1−t)Λ0+tΛ1V_tV_t^\top=(1-t)\Lambda_0+t\Lambda_1Vt​Vt⊤​=(1−t)Λ0​+tΛ1​, while the squared Frobenius distance is the same convex combination. Taking the infimum proves joint convexity.

Remark: Diagonal covariances and Hellinger geometry

If Σα=diag⁡(ri)i\cov_\al=\diag(r_i)_iΣα​=diag(ri​)i​ and Σβ=diag⁡(si)i\cov_\be=\diag(s_i)_iΣβ​=diag(si​)i​ are diagonal, the Bures metric reduces to the Euclidean distance between square roots,

B(Σα,Σβ)=∥r−s∥2.\Bb(\cov_\al,\cov_\be)=\norm{\sqrt r-\sqrt s}_2.B(Σα​,Σβ​)=∥∥​r​−s​∥∥​2​.
(81)

This is the finite-dimensional Hellinger geometry on the nonnegative covariance coordinates: variances are compared after the amplitude change of variables ri↦rir_i\mapsto\sqrt{r_i}ri​↦ri​​.

References¶
  1. Monge, G. (1781). Mémoire sur la théorie des déblais et des remblais. Histoire de l’Académie Royale Des Sciences, 666–704.
  2. Villani, C. (2003). Topics in Optimal Transportation (Vol. 58). American Mathematical Society.
  3. Villani, C. (2009). Optimal Transport: Old and New (Vol. 338). Springer.
  4. Santambrogio, F. (2015). Optimal Transport for Applied Mathematicians: Calculus of Variations, PDEs, and Modeling. Birkhäuser.
  5. Rachev, S. T., & Rüschendorf, L. (1998). Mass Transportation Problems: Volume I: Theory. Springer.
  6. Rudin, W. (1987). Real and Complex Analysis (Third). McGraw–Hill.
  7. Bogachev, V. I. (2007). Measure Theory. Springer.
  8. Reinhard, E., Adhikhmin, M., Gooch, B., & Shirley, P. (2001). Color Transfer between Images. IEEE Computer Graphics and Applications, 21(5), 34–41.
  9. Pitié, F., Kokaram, A. C., & Dahyot, R. (2005). N-dimensional Probability Density Function Transfer and Its Application to Color Transfer. IEEE International Conference on Computer Vision, 1434–1439.
  10. Rabin, J., Peyré, G., Delon, J., & Bernot, M. (2011). Wasserstein barycenter and its application to texture mixing. International Conference on Scale Space and Variational Methods in Computer Vision, 435–446.
  11. Brenier, Y. (1987). Décomposition polaire et réarrangement monotone des champs de vecteurs. C. R. Acad. Sci. Paris Sér. I Math., 305(19), 805–808.
  12. Brenier, Y. (1991). Polar factorization and monotone rearrangement of vector-valued functions. Communications on Pure and Applied Mathematics, 44(4), 375–417.
  13. Gangbo, W., & McCann, R. J. (1996). The geometry of optimal transportation. Acta Mathematica, 177(2), 113–161.
  14. McCann, R. J. (1997). A convexity principle for interacting gases. Advances in Mathematics, 128(1), 153–179.
  15. Caffarelli, L. (2003). The Monge-Ampere equation and optimal transportation, an elementary review. Lecture Notes in Mathematics, Springer-Verlag, 1–10.
